requirements.problem_statement: Build a chat application similar to WhatsApp.

user_input: Build a chat app like WhatsApp with features like text messaging, media sharing, voice and video calls, and group chats. Security can be medium (no end-to-end encryption required). The budget is low and the scale is medium (targeting around 1M users).

modules: Real-time Signaling Service (WebSockets for Presence & Messaging)
Media Relay Service (TURN/STUN for Voice/Video Calls)
architecture.reasoning: A WhatsApp-like application requires low-latency, real-time communication. Microservices allow independent scaling of the signaling and media relay services, which handle high-concurrency connections. A modular approach enables rapid development within the 3-month timeline using managed services for media storage and signaling.

frontend.reasoning: Enables cross-platform development (iOS/Android) within the 3-month timeline while maintaining native-like performance.

backend.reasoning: Fastify provides high performance and low overhead, crucial for latency requirements. TypeScript improves maintainability for a 3-month timeline.

database.name: PostgreSQL (User/Metadata) & Redis (Chat/Presence)
database.reasoning: PostgreSQL handles structured user/relationship data reliably. Redis is essential for low-latency real-time chat caching and presence tracking.

infra.reasoning: Robust managed services (S3, CloudFront, ElastiCache) reduce operational overhead and meet 99.9% availability requirements within a low budget.

tech_stack.overall_reasoning: The stack prioritizes developer velocity and managed services to meet the tight 3-month deadline. AWS AppSync provides a managed real-time signaling backbone, reducing complexity. PostgreSQL and Redis provide the necessary balance of data integrity and low-latency access required for messaging. Coturn on EC2 is the most cost-effective solution for media relay.

services.name: API Gateway
services.responsibility: Handles incoming traffic, authentication, and routing requests to appropriate microservices.

services.name: User Service
services.responsibility: Manages user profiles, contact lists, and authentication metadata.

services.name: Chat Service
services.responsibility: Handles text messaging, group chat logic, and message persistence.

services.name: Media Service
services.responsibility: Manages media uploads, storage, and distribution via CloudFront.

services.name: Real-time Signaling Service
services.responsibility: Facilitates real-time delivery of messages and online/offline status updates.

services.name: Media Relay Service
services.responsibility: Provides TURN/STUN relay infrastructure for voice/video call signaling and traffic relay.

services.name: Notification Service
services.responsibility: Handles push notifications for offline users and call alerts.

services.reasoning: The system is designed as a set of decoupled microservices to handle high-concurrency real-time demands. The API Gateway manages entry and authentication, while specialized services handle message routing, media handling, and real-time signaling. This architecture aligns with the 3-month timeline by leveraging managed AWS services and clear separation of concerns.

database_schema.reasoning: The schema uses PostgreSQL for persistent relational data (users, contacts, media metadata, messages) and assumes Redis for transient state (presence, active sessions). PostgreSQL tables are normalized to support the User, Chat, and Media services, while Redis handles high-frequency signaling data.

api_design.name: User Service API
api_design.purpose: Manage user profiles and contact lists.

api_design.name: Chat Service API
api_design.purpose: Handle message persistence and conversation management.

api_design.name: Media Service API
api_design.purpose: Handle media uploads via signed URLs and metadata management.

api_design.name: Notification Service API
api_design.purpose: Manage push notification settings and preferences.

api_endpoints.frontend_notes:
1. Use WebSocket/AppSync for real-time message updates.
2. Implement client-side retry logic for media uploads using S3 signed URLs.
3. Use JWT tokens in the Authorization header for all requests.
4. Client should maintain a local cache of user presence status.
api_endpoints.reasoning: The API design follows a RESTful pattern for standard CRUD operations while offloading real-time traffic to specialized WebSocket/Pub-Sub channels. This approach minimizes latency and ensures the system remains scalable for 1M users.

infra_services: Amazon RDS for PostgreSQL (User Service/Chat metadata)
infrastructure.deployment: Containerized (ECS Fargate) with managed AWS services for high availability and low operational overhead.
infrastructure.reasoning: To support 1M users with a low budget and 3-month timeline, the architecture prioritizes managed AWS services (Fargate, RDS, AppSync) to reduce infrastructure maintenance. ECS Fargate provides auto-scaling for microservices. AppSync efficiently handles WebSocket state for real-time signaling, and CloudFront + S3 ensures sub-second media delivery. Media Relay (TURN/STUN) is deployed on EC2 to handle high-bandwidth traffic requirements cost-effectively.

api_endpoints.frontend_notes:
1. Use WebSocket/AppSync for real-time message updates.
2. Implement client-side retry logic for media uploads using S3 signed URLs.
3. Use JWT tokens in the Authorization header for all requests.
4. Client should maintain a local cache of user presence status.
api_endpoints.reasoning: The API design follows a RESTful pattern for standard CRUD operations while offloading real-time traffic to specialized WebSocket/Pub-Sub channels. This approach minimizes latency and ensures the system remains scalable for 1M users.

issues.description: The Real-time Signaling Service is listed as having a dependency on the Chat Service, creating a circular dependency or an inverted control flow for a core real-time messaging pipeline, as the Chat Service also depends on the Signaling Service.

The architecture lacks a specialized Call/Session Management service to handle call state (ringing, active, busy), call history, and cross-service coordination between signaling, media relay, and user presence for voice/video features.

The Chat Service owns 'Messages' and 'Conversation state' data, but there is no explicit mention of how message delivery receipts (read/delivered status) are synchronized or persisted across sessions, which is critical for WhatsApp-like functionality.

The architecture proposes both a custom 'Real-time Signaling Service (WebSockets)' and 'AWS AppSync (Managed WebSocket/Real-time)'. Utilizing both creates redundant infrastructure, increasing latency overhead, management complexity, and development time, contradicting the 3-month timeline and low-budget constraints.

Hosting Coturn on EC2 for media relay introduces a single point of failure and manual scaling challenges that conflict with the 99.9% availability requirement. For a 'low budget' and 3-month timeline, managing TURN/STUN infrastructure manually is a high-risk operational burden.

The design lacks a clear strategy for long-term chat storage. Relying on Redis for chat data without an explicit cold-storage/archival mechanism in PostgreSQL or NoSQL will lead to memory exhaustion or data loss as the user base grows toward 1M, violating the scale constraint.

The API design defines a Notification Service, but there is no corresponding persistence layer defined in the database schema (e.g., notification_preferences or notification_logs tables) to support this service.

The Chat Service API is expected to handle 'conversation management', yet the database schema lacks a 'conversations' table to group messages, only providing a 'conversation_id' foreign key in the 'messages' table without a parent entity.

issues.issue_name: Single Database Bottleneck for Chat Metadata
issues.description: The infrastructure design utilizes Amazon RDS for both User Service and Chat metadata. Given the 1M user scale, a single RDS instance (or even a standard multi-AZ deployment) risks becoming a bottleneck for high-frequency chat metadata writes. There is no partitioning or read-replica strategy defined for the chat metadata, creating a high availability and performance risk.

issues.issue_name: Lack of Auto-scaling for Media Relay EC2 Instances
issues.description: Media Relay (TURN/STUN) is deployed on EC2 instances. While ECS Fargate is used for other microservices, the infrastructure lacks a defined auto-scaling group or load balancing strategy for these EC2-based media relays, creating a significant availability risk during traffic spikes.

The API design requires the client to maintain a local cache of user presence, while the infrastructure uses ElastiCache for Redis. There is no explicit mechanism defined for cache invalidation or synchronization between the Redis presence store and the client-side cache, leading to potential stale data and inconsistent user experience during high-concurrency scenarios.

suggestions.title: Asynchronous Write-Behind Caching for Chat History
suggestions.description: Implement Redis-based 'Write-Behind' caching for chat history. Instead of synchronous database writes, push chat messages to a Redis stream or list, then use a background worker to batch insert into PostgreSQL. This reduces latency for the sender and lowers RDS load.

suggestions.title: Database Partitioning for Chat Messages
suggestions.description: Shift from standard PostgreSQL to a partitioned table strategy based on 'chat_id' or 'date' for chat message storage. This prevents performance degradation as the message volume grows toward 1M users.

suggestions.title: Optimize Media Relay Infrastructure Costs
suggestions.description: For the Media Relay (TURN/STUN), instead of custom EC2 instances, explore AWS Global Accelerator combined with managed TURN services or spot instances with auto-scaling to avoid over-provisioning during off-peak hours.

suggestions.title: Implement gRPC for Internal Microservice Communication
suggestions.description: Standardize API communication between microservices using gRPC/Protobuf instead of REST for internal traffic. This reduces payload size, improves serialization speed, and enforces strict schema contracts, reducing integration bugs in the 3-month timeline.

suggestions.title: Optimized Presence Heartbeat Pattern
suggestions.description: Implement a 'Presence Heartbeat' optimization in Redis. Instead of updating presence on every event, use a sliding window approach with a short TTL, reducing Redis write operations significantly while maintaining near-real-time accuracy.

final_output_report:
# System Design Report

### User Input
Build a chat app like WhatsApp with features like text messaging, media sharing, voice and video calls, and group chats. Security can be medium (no end-to-end encryption required). The budget is low and the scale is medium (targeting around 1M users).

## Requirements

### Problem Statement
Build a chat application similar to WhatsApp.

### Target Users
- general users

### Core Features
- text messaging
- media sharing
- voice calls
- video calls
- group chats

### User Actions
- send text messages
- share media
- initiate voice calls
- initiate video calls
- create/manage group chats

### Entities
- user
- message
- media
- call
- group

### Scale Hint


### Domain
chat application

## Architecture

**Pattern**: microservices

### Modules
- API Gateway (Load Balancing & Auth)
- User Service (Profile & Relationship Management)
- Chat Service (Text Messaging & Group Logic)
- Media Service (Storage & CDN Integration)
- Real-time Signaling Service (WebSockets for Presence & Messaging)
- Media Relay Service (TURN/STUN for Voice/Video Calls)
- Notification Service (Push Notifications)

**Reasoning**: A WhatsApp-like application requires low-latency, real-time communication. Microservices allow independent scaling of the signaling and media relay services, which handle high-concurrency connections. A modular approach enables rapid development within the 3-month timeline using managed services for media storage and signaling.

**Confidence**: 0.95

## Tech Stack

**Frontend**
- Language: TypeScript
- Framework: React Native
- Reasoning: Enables cross-platform development (iOS/Android) within the 3-month timeline while maintaining native-like performance.

**Backend**
- Language: TypeScript
- Framework: Node.js (Fastify)
- API Style: REST & WebSocket
- Reasoning: Fastify provides high performance and low overhead, crucial for latency requirements. TypeScript improves maintainability for a 3-month timeline.

**Database**
- Type: Relational/Key-Value
- Name: PostgreSQL (User/Metadata) & Redis (Chat/Presence)
- ORM: Prisma
- Reasoning: PostgreSQL handles structured user/relationship data reliably. Redis is essential for low-latency real-time chat caching and presence tracking.

**Infrastructure**
- Provider: AWS
- Services:
  - Amazon ECS (Fargate)
  - Amazon ElastiCache (Redis)
  - Amazon RDS (PostgreSQL)
  - Amazon S3 + CloudFront
  - Amazon SNS (Push Notifications)
  - AWS AppSync (Managed WebSocket/Real-time)
  - Coturn (Open Source for TURN/STUN)
- Reasoning: Robust managed services (S3, CloudFront, ElastiCache) reduce operational overhead and meet 99.9% availability requirements within a low budget.

**Overall Reasoning**
The stack prioritizes developer velocity and managed services to meet the tight 3-month deadline. AWS AppSync provides a managed real-time signaling backbone, reducing complexity. PostgreSQL and Redis provide the necessary balance of data integrity and low-latency access required for messaging. Coturn on EC2 is the most cost-effective solution for media relay.

**Confidence**
0.9

## Design Bundle

### Services
- Name: API Gateway
  - Responsibility: Handles incoming traffic, authentication, and routing requests to appropriate microservices.
  - Owned Data:
    - N/A
  - Dependencies:
    - None
  - Key Patterns: ['JWT for Auth', 'Rate Limiting', 'Load Balancing']
- Name: User Service
  - Responsibility: Manages user profiles, contact lists, and authentication metadata.
  - Owned Data:
    - User Profile
    - Relationships/Contacts
  - Dependencies:
    - API Gateway
  - Key Patterns: ['Prisma ORM']
- Name: Chat Service
  - Responsibility: Handles text messaging, group chat logic, and message persistence.
  - Owned Data:
    - Messages
    - Group metadata
    - Conversation state
  - Dependencies:
    - API Gateway
    - Real-time Signaling Service
  - Key Patterns: ['Redis Pub/Sub', 'WebSocket integration']
- Name: Media Service
  - Responsibility: Manages media uploads, storage, and distribution via CloudFront.
  - Owned Data:
    - Media metadata
  - Dependencies:
    - API Gateway
  - Key Patterns: ['S3 Signed URLs', 'CDN caching']
- Name: Real-time Signaling Service
  - Responsibility: Facilitates real-time delivery of messages and online/offline status updates.
  - Owned Data:
    - User Presence status
  - Dependencies:
    - Chat Service
  - Key Patterns: ['AWS AppSync', 'Redis Presence Tracking']
- Name: Media Relay Service
  - Responsibility: Provides TURN/STUN relay infrastructure for voice/video call signaling and traffic relay.
  - Owned Data:
    - N/A
  - Dependencies:
    - None
  - Key Patterns: ['ICE/STUN/TURN protocol']
- Name: Notification Service
  - Responsibility: Handles push notifications for offline users and call alerts.
  - Owned Data:
    - N/A
  - Dependencies:
    - User Service
    - Chat Service
  - Key Patterns: ['AWS SNS integration']
- Reasoning: The system is designed as a set of decoupled microservices to handle high-concurrency real-time demands. The API Gateway manages entry and authentication, while specialized services handle message routing, media handling, and real-time signaling. This architecture aligns with the 3-month timeline by leveraging managed AWS services and clear separation of concerns.
- Confidence: 0.95

### Database Schema
- Name: users
  - Description: User Service: Stores core user account information.
  - Fields:
    - id: uuid, pk
    - email: varchar(255), unique
    - password_hash: varchar(255)
    - display_name: varchar(100)
    - created_at: timestamp
  - Relations:
    - None
- Name: contacts
  - Description: User Service: Manages bidirectional contact relationships.
  - Fields:
    - id: uuid, pk
    - user_id: uuid, fk
    - contact_id: uuid, fk
    - status: varchar(20)
    - created_at: timestamp
  - Relations:
    - contacts.user_id -> users.id
    - contacts.contact_id -> users.id
- Name: messages
  - Description: Chat Service: Stores message history.
  - Fields:
    - id: uuid, pk
    - conversation_id: uuid
    - sender_id: uuid, fk
    - content: text
    - created_at: timestamp
  - Relations:
    - messages.sender_id -> users.id
- Name: groups
  - Description: Chat Service: Stores metadata for group chats.
  - Fields:
    - id: uuid, pk
    - name: varchar(100)
    - created_at: timestamp
  - Relations:
    - None
- Name: group_members
  - Description: Chat Service: Maps users to groups.
  - Fields:
    - id: uuid, pk
    - group_id: uuid, fk
    - user_id: uuid, fk
    - role: varchar(20)
  - Relations:
    - group_members.group_id -> groups.id
    - group_members.user_id -> users.id
- Name: media_metadata
  - Description: Media Service: Stores metadata for uploaded files.
  - Fields:
    - id: uuid, pk
    - uploader_id: uuid, fk
    - s3_key: varchar(512)
    - file_type: varchar(50)
    - created_at: timestamp
  - Relations:
    - media_metadata.uploader_id -> users.id
- Name: redis_presence_tracking
  - Description: Real-time Signaling Service: Redis key-value structure for presence.
  - Fields:
    - user_id: uuid, pk
    - status: varchar(20)
    - last_seen: timestamp
  - Relations:
    - None
- Reasoning: The schema uses PostgreSQL for persistent relational data (users, contacts, media metadata, messages) and assumes Redis for transient state (presence, active sessions). PostgreSQL tables are normalized to support the User, Chat, and Media services, while Redis handles high-frequency signaling data.
- Confidence: 0.95

### API Endpoints
- Name: User Service API
  - Purpose: Manage user profiles and contact lists.
  - Base Route: /users
- Name: Chat Service API
  - Purpose: Handle message persistence and conversation management.
  - Base Route: /chats
- Name: Media Service API
  - Purpose: Handle media uploads via signed URLs and metadata management.
  - Base Route: /media
- Name: Notification Service API
  - Purpose: Manage push notification settings and preferences.
  - Base Route: /notifications
- Frontend Notes: 1. Use WebSocket/AppSync for real-time message updates.
2. Implement client-side retry logic for media uploads using S3 signed URLs.
3. Use JWT tokens in the Authorization header for all requests.
4. Client should maintain a local cache of user presence status.
- Reasoning: The API design follows a RESTful pattern for standard CRUD operations while offloading real-time traffic to specialized WebSocket/Pub-Sub channels. This approach minimizes latency and ensures the system remains scalable for 1M users.
- Confidence: 0.95

### Infrastructure
- Cloud: AWS
- Services:
  - N/A
- Deployment: Containerized (ECS Fargate) with managed AWS services for high availability and low operational overhead.
- Reasoning: To support 1M users with a low budget and 3-month timeline, the architecture prioritizes managed AWS services (Fargate, RDS, AppSync) to reduce infrastructure maintenance. ECS Fargate provides auto-scaling for microservices. AppSync efficiently handles WebSocket state for real-time signaling, and CloudFront + S3 ensures sub-second media delivery. Media Relay (TURN/STUN) is deployed on EC2 to handle high-bandwidth traffic requirements cost-effectively.
- Confidence: 0.95

**Final Score**: 0.7292%
